stories.title: Aesop's Fables
stories.author: Aesop
stories.summary: Short moral tales featuring talking animals — the fountainhead of Western storytelling.
stories.description: A collection of hundreds of tiny stories, most just a few hundred words long, featuring animals and the occasional human who meet each other on the road and teach one another a lesson. These are the fables that gave us 'sour grapes,' 'slow and steady wins the race,' and 'a friend in need is a friend indeed.' Perfect for bedtime because each one is complete in itself — read one, or five, and stop whenever sleep comes.
stories.authorBio: Aesop is the legendary author of the world's most famous collection of fables. Whether he was a real person — possibly a Greek storyteller and slave living in the 6th century BCE — or a name later attached to a tradition of folk wisdom, the fables themselves have shaped storytelling for more than two thousand years.
stories.relevance: A child's first taste of stories that come with a quiet lesson tucked inside. Short enough for a tired parent and a tireder child, and they leave room to talk afterward.

stories.title: Just So Stories
stories.author: Rudyard Kipling
stories.summary: Kipling's playful 'how and why' tales, written to be read aloud to a child.
stories.description: Twelve invented origin stories — how the whale got his throat, how the camel got his hump, how the leopard got his spots — written by Kipling for his own daughter Josephine. The prose has the lilt of a story told aloud, with repeated phrases ('O Best Beloved') that beg to be chanted in rhythm. Each tale stands alone at twenty or thirty minutes, so the book is perfect for a chapter-a-night pace.
stories.authorBio: Rudyard Kipling (1865–1936) was a British author and poet born in Bombay during the Raj. He wrote The Jungle Book, Kim, and Just So Stories, and in 1907 became the first English-language writer to win the Nobel Prize in Literature. His best work for children carries the warmth of stories invented at bedtime for his own family.
stories.relevance: Composed to be read aloud. The cadence, the made-up words, the asides to 'O Best Beloved' — it's built for a parent's voice. Reading them is genuinely good practice for everything else.

stories.title: The Wind in the Willows
stories.author: Kenneth Grahame
stories.summary: Mole, Ratty, Mr. Toad, and a great golden afternoon on an English riverbank.
stories.description: Kenneth Grahame's quiet, contented book about friendship and the deep pleasure of small things. Mole throws down his spring-cleaning brush, meets the Water Rat, and discovers boats, picnics, the Wild Wood, and the incorrigible Mr Toad — whose unfortunate passions for gypsy caravans and (later) motor cars drive the plot of the middle chapters. It is one of the gentlest and most contented books in English children's literature.
stories.authorBio: Kenneth Grahame (1859–1932) was a Scottish writer who spent his working life as Secretary of the Bank of England. He wrote The Wind in the Willows for his young son Alastair, originally as bedtime stories and then as letters when Alastair was away. It was published in 1908 and rejected by several editors before becoming one of the most beloved books in the English language.
stories.relevance: A book about friendship, the seasons, and the deep pleasure of small things — a riverbank, a picnic, a quiet afternoon. Perfect for the slow chapter-a-night pace bedtime asks for.

stories.title: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland
stories.author: Lewis Carroll
stories.summary: A small girl follows a waistcoated rabbit down a hole and falls into a world where nothing makes sense except everything.
stories.description: Lewis Carroll's Wonderland came from a boat trip up the Thames with three little girls, one of whom — Alice Liddell — asked him to write it down afterward. Alice falls down a rabbit-hole and meets a White Rabbit, a Caterpillar on a mushroom, a Mock Turtle, a Cheshire Cat, and a Queen who shouts 'Off with her head!' at regular intervals. The book lives in the rhythm of its sentences — patient, absurd, quietly brilliant.
stories.authorBio: Lewis Carroll was the pen name of Charles Lutwidge Dodgson (1832–1898), an English mathematician, photographer, and Anglican deacon at Christ Church, Oxford. He invented the Wonderland stories on that boat trip for the daughters of his friend the dean, and wrote them down because Alice asked him to.
stories.relevance: One of those books that lives in the rhythm of the sentences. It rewards being read aloud slowly, with patience for the jokes that work on grown-ups too.

stories.title: The Jungle Book
stories.author: Rudyard Kipling
stories.summary: Mowgli, a human child raised by wolves, grows up among the creatures of the Seeonee hills.
stories.description: Kipling's most cinematic book, written in Vermont in 1894 from the India of his memory. Mowgli the man-cub is taken in by a family of wolves on the night of a Council in the hills. Over seven chapters he is taught the Law of the Jungle by Baloo the bear and Bagheera the black panther, is kidnapped by the Bandar-log, confronts Shere Khan the tiger, and finally returns to the world of men. The chapters rotate between Mowgli's story and other tales of India — the white seal, the little mongoose Rikki-Tikki-Tavi, the ghost elephant Toomai.
stories.authorBio: Rudyard Kipling (1865–1936) was born in Bombay and spent his early childhood in India before being sent to England for school. He wrote The Jungle Book in Vermont in 1894, drawing on the India of his memory. The stories of Mowgli have shaped how generations have imagined the jungle, for better and for worse — they reward reading with a parent's eye.
stories.relevance: Kipling at his most cinematic. Clear stakes, fierce love between Mother Wolf and Mowgli, long ceremonial scenes that read beautifully aloud. Better for slightly older children — there is danger, and a tiger.

stories.title: The Secret Garden
stories.author: Frances Hodgson Burnett
stories.summary: An orphaned girl, a walled garden left to grow wild, and a boy who believes he cannot walk.
stories.description: Mary Lennox is orphaned in colonial India and dispatched across the world to a great draughty house on the Yorkshire moors belonging to a hunchbacked uncle she has never met. She is sour and lonely when she arrives, but the moors — and a walled garden left untouched for ten years — begin to change her. One of the great quiet healing books of children's literature, about weather and gardens and a child who is allowed to be cross.
stories.authorBio: Frances Hodgson Burnett (1849–1924) was an English-American novelist who also wrote A Little Princess and Little Lord Fauntleroy. She moved between England and the United States all her life, and the moors and walled gardens of her childhood Manchester thread through The Secret Garden, written in 1911.
stories.relevance: A long, slow, healing book — about gardens and weather and a child who is allowed to be cross. Pairs well with rainy nights and tea.
